title: Introduction
text: You've learned how to project graphs and why structure matters. Now, let's put algorithms to work. In this lesson, you'll run algorithms from each of the five categories and see how they answer different questions about the same data. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-6-run-algorithms-s103-000-new] As you can see here, we have a diagram illustrating the five categories of graph algorithms: Centrality, Community Detection, Similarity, Pathfinding, and Node Embeddings. Each category is represented by a distinct node cluster, with arrows indicating the types of questions they address.

title: Algorithm details
text: Don't worry too much about retaining the specific details of each algorithm right now. Later in the course, we'll dive into each of them in much greater detail.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to run algorithms from each of the five GDS categories on projected graphs. You'll recognize how different centrality algorithms define 'importance' differently and apply community detection, similarity, pathfinding, and embedding algorithms. Plus, you'll be able to choose the right algorithm category based on your analytical question.

title: Setup: Create the Actor Network
text: First, let's create an actor collaboration network. Using the Cypher query, you'll match actors who have acted in the same movie and project this into a graph. You should see approximately 36,000 nodes and over 1 million relationships.

title: Category 1: Centrality
text: Centrality algorithms rank nodes by importance, but 'important' depends on how you define it. Let's try three definitions on the same graph. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-6-run-algorithms-s107-003-new] Notice how this illustration depicts three types of centrality: degree, PageRank, and betweenness, each shown on a separate graph with nodes sized according to their centrality measure.

title: Degree Centrality
text: The question here is, 'Who has the most direct connections?' Degree Centrality counts outgoing relationships. After running the query, note the top actor and their score.

title: PageRank
text: Now, let's ask, 'Who is connected to other important nodes?' PageRank considers both the connection count and the importance of those connections. Compare the rankings. Do the same actors appear at the top?

title: Betweenness Centrality
text: The question here is, 'Who bridges different groups?' Betweenness measures how often a node appears on shortest paths between others. Note that betweenness is computationally expensive, with O(n³) complexity, so this may take a moment.

title: Three Definitions of "Important"
text: Using the same graph, we get three different answers: Degree shows the most collaborations, PageRank highlights connections to influential actors, and Betweenness identifies nodes that bridge different communities. The algorithm you choose defines what 'importance' means.

title: Category 2: Community Detection
text: Community detection finds clusters—nodes more connected to each other than to the rest of the network. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-6-run-algorithms-s112-000-existing] As you can see, the graph on the left shows a raw network, while the right side highlights identified communities, each with a different color.

title: Louvain
text: The Louvain algorithm groups nodes by maximizing 'modularity'—the connection density within groups versus between groups. After running the query, check how many communities there are and which is the largest. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-6-run-algorithms-s113-008-new] This flowchart illustrates the Louvain algorithm process, from initial random grouping to iterative modularity optimization.

title: Explore a Community
text: Let's see who's in the largest community. These actors are more densely connected to each other than to the rest of the network. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-6-run-algorithms-s114-009-new] Notice how this illustration highlights a densely connected community within the network.

title: Tuning: maxLevels
text: Louvain builds hierarchical communities, and the `maxLevels` parameter controls the depth. More levels result in fewer, larger communities with higher modularity.

title: Category 3: Similarity
text: Node Similarity finds nodes with similar connection patterns. For this, we need a bipartite projection. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-6-run-algorithms-s116-011-new] Here, you can see a bipartite graph projection showing user and movie nodes, illustrating node similarity based on shared connections.

title: Run Node Similarity
text: Let's find users with similar movie tastes. High similarity indicates that users have rated many of the same movies.

title: Category 4: Pathfinding
text: Dijkstra's Shortest Path algorithm finds the shortest path between two nodes. For example, find the degrees of separation between actors like Kevin Bacon and Meg Ryan. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-6-run-algorithms-s118-013-new] This flowchart shows the steps Dijkstra's algorithm takes to find the shortest path between two nodes in a graph.

title: Category 5: Node Embeddings
text: FastRP creates vector representations that capture each node's network position. Each actor now has a 64-dimensional vector. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-6-run-algorithms-s119-014-new] This illustration shows how FastRP generates node embeddings, with example vectors for selected nodes.

title: Using Embeddings
text: Let's find actors structurally similar to Kevin Bacon. These actors occupy similar positions in the collaboration network.

title: Cleanup
text: Before moving on, make sure to drop the projections. This will help keep your workspace clean and organized.

title: Quick Reference
text: Here's a quick reference to help you choose the right algorithm: If you're asking 'Who is influential?', try Centrality with PageRank, Degree, or Betweenness. For 'What clusters exist?', use Community Detection with Louvain or Leiden. To find 'Who has similar patterns?', use Similarity with Node Similarity. For 'How are nodes connected?', Pathfinding with Dijkstra is your go-to. And for machine learning applications, use Embeddings with FastRP.

title: Summary
text: You've now applied algorithms from all five categories: Centrality, with three definitions of 'important'; Community Detection, finding clusters with Louvain; Similarity, identifying users with similar movie tastes; Pathfinding, exploring degrees of separation; and Embeddings, creating vector representations for machine learning. The same graph can answer different questions depending on which algorithm you run. In the next lesson, you'll learn about the execution modes that control how results are returned and stored.
